Repos (A) is for version controlling notebooks but doesn't manage job definitions or environments as code. Manual UI creation (B) is error-prone and not reproducible. Terraform (D) is a valid alternative but DAB is Databricks' native recommended solution.

questions.text: Which top-level key in `databricks.yml` is the ONLY required field?

options.A: `targets`
options.B: `resources`
options.C: `bundle`
options.D: `workspace`

questions.explanation: The `bundle` key is the only required top-level mapping in `databricks.yml`, and within it, the `name` field is required. All other keys — `targets`, `resources`, `workspace`, `variables`, `permissions` — are optional, though `targets` is strongly recommended for multi-environment deployments.

questions.text: A data engineer is writing a `databricks.yml` file and wants to define three environments: `dev`, `staging`, and `prod`. Which top-level key should they use to define these environments?

options.A: `environments`
options.B: `targets`
options.C: `workspaces`
options.D: `deployments`

questions.explanation: The `targets` key defines deployment target contexts. Each entry under `targets` represents a named environment with its own workspace settings, resource overrides, variables, and deployment mode. The key was previously called `environments` in older DAB versions, but the current (exam-relevant) name is `targets`.

questions.text: In a `databricks.yml` file, a data engineer defines the following: ```yaml targets: dev: default: true mode: development prod: mode: production ``` What does `default: true` indicate on the `dev` target?

options.A: The dev target is the only target that can be deployed
options.B: When running `databricks bundle deploy` without specifying a `--target`, the dev target is used automatically
options.C: The dev target uses the default Databricks runtime version
options.D: The dev target deploys to the default Databricks workspace configured in the CLI profile

questions.explanation: Setting `default: true` on a target means it is used when no `--target` flag is provided to CLI commands. Only one target can be set as default. It does not restrict which targets can be deployed (A), has nothing to do with runtime versions (C), or specifically mean the CLI profile workspace (D).

questions.text: Which section of `databricks.yml` is used to specify the Workspace URL and file paths for the bundle deployment?

options.A: `bundle.host`
options.B: `workspace`
options.C: `resources.workspace`
options.D: `targets.workspace_url`

questions.explanation: The `workspace` section defines connection details including `host` (URL), `profile`, `root_path`, `artifact_path`, `file_path`, and `state_path`. It can appear at the top level (default for all targets) and can be overridden per target within the `targets` section.

questions.text: A data engineer writes the following in `databricks.yml`: ```yaml workspace: root_path: /Workspace/Users/${workspace.current_user.userName}/.bundle/${bundle.name}/${bundle.target} ``` What type of syntax is `${bundle.name}` and `${bundle.target}`?

options.A: Python f-string interpolation
options.B: Jinja2 template variables
options.C: Built-in DAB substitution variables
options.D: Environment variable references

questions.explanation: The `${}` syntax in `databricks.yml` is DAB's built-in substitution system. Built-in substitutions include `${bundle.name}`, `${bundle.target}`, `${workspace.host}`, `${workspace.current_user.userName}`, and others. They are resolved at deploy time. Custom variables use `${var.variable_name}`. This is neither Python f-strings (A), Jinja2 (B), nor OS environment variables (D).

questions.text: What is the correct syntax to reference a custom variable named `catalog_name` inside a `databricks.yml` file?

options.A: `$catalog_name`
options.B: `$(var.catalog_name)`
options.C: `${var.catalog_name}`
options.D: `{{var.catalog_name}}`

questions.explanation: Custom variables defined in the `variables` section are referenced using the `${var.<variable_name>}` syntax. Built-in substitutions use `${<path>}` without the `var.` prefix (e.g., `${bundle.name}`). The other syntaxes are not valid in DAB YAML.

questions.text: A data engineer wants to split their bundle configuration across multiple YAML files for better organization. Which top-level key in `databricks.yml` enables this?

options.A: `extends`
options.B: `import`
options.C: `include`
options.D: `merge`

questions.explanation: The `include` key accepts a list of glob patterns pointing to additional YAML configuration files. Example: `include: - resources/*.yml`. All included files are merged at deploy time. This key can only be used at the top level, meaning included resources are available to all targets.

questions.text: A data engineer runs `databricks bundle validate`. What does this command do?

options.A: Deploys the bundle to the default target workspace
options.B: Runs all unit tests defined in the bundle
options.C: Verifies the bundle configuration syntax against schema and outputs a summary of the bundle identity
options.D: Creates a new bundle from a template

questions.explanation: `bundle validate` checks that the `databricks.yml` configuration is syntactically correct and valid against the resource schemas. It outputs the bundle name, target, workspace host, user, and path — but it does NOT deploy anything (A), run tests (B), or create a new bundle (D).

questions.text: Which command would a data engineer use to deploy a bundle to the `prod` target?

options.A: `databricks bundle run --target prod`
options.B: `databricks bundle deploy --target prod`
options.C: `databricks bundle publish prod`
options.D: `databricks bundle push --env prod`

questions.explanation: `databricks bundle deploy --target <name>` deploys the bundle to the specified target. The `--target` flag (or `-t`) accepts the target name as defined in the `targets` section. `bundle run` (A) triggers an already-deployed job/pipeline. `bundle publish` and `bundle push` are not valid commands.

questions.text: What is the purpose of `databricks bundle plan`?

options.A: It creates a deployment plan file for manual review
options.B: It shows what changes would be made by `bundle deploy` without actually deploying anything
options.C: It schedules the bundle deployment to run at a specific time
options.D: It generates a Terraform plan for the bundle resources

questions.explanation: `bundle plan` is a preview/dry-run command. It shows which resources would be created, updated, or deleted if you ran `bundle deploy` — without making any actual changes. This is useful for reviewing impacts before deployment. It is not a scheduling tool (C) and has nothing to do with Terraform (D).

questions.text: A data engineer wants to permanently remove all deployed resources (jobs, pipelines, artifacts) for a bundle. Which command should they use?

options.A: `databricks bundle delete --all`
options.B: `databricks bundle undeploy`
options.C: `databricks bundle destroy`
options.D: `databricks bundle remove --target prod`

questions.explanation: `databricks bundle destroy` permanently deletes all previously-deployed jobs, pipelines, clusters, and artifacts associated with the bundle in the target workspace. This action **cannot be undone**. It is the correct command for full teardown. The other options are not valid DAB commands.

questions.text: A data engineer has an existing job in their Databricks workspace and wants to bring it under DAB management. Which command generates a YAML configuration from an existing workspace resource?

options.A: `databricks bundle import job --existing-job-id 12345`
options.B: `databricks bundle generate job --existing-job-id 12345`
options.C: `databricks bundle export --resource job`
options.D: `databricks bundle init --from-job 12345`

questions.explanation: `databricks bundle generate job --existing-job-id <id>` (or `--existing-job-name`) auto-generates a YAML resource definition from an existing workspace job and places it in the bundle configuration. This is the migration path from manually-created jobs to DAB management. `bundle import` is not a valid command.

questions.text: How does Databricks Asset Bundles (DAB) differ from Databricks Repos?

options.A: DAB manages notebook version control while Repos manages job deployments
options.B: Repos syncs Git content (notebooks, code) into the workspace; DAB defines and deploys the entire project including jobs, pipelines, and infrastructure as code
options.C: DAB requires Unity Catalog while Repos works with Hive Metastore
options.D: They are interchangeable — both provide the same functionality

questions.explanation: Databricks Repos is a Git integration feature that syncs code (especially notebooks) from a Git repo into the Databricks workspace. It does not manage job definitions, cluster configs, or environment promotions. DAB manages the full project deployment — code AND infrastructure (jobs, pipelines, permissions, environment targeting). They are complementary: DAB can deploy code to workspace Repos paths, but their purposes are distinct. They are not interchangeable (D).

questions.text: A team has been creating Databricks jobs manually through the UI in each environment. What is a major disadvantage of this approach compared to using Databricks Asset Bundles?

options.A: Manual jobs run slower than DAB-deployed jobs
options.B: Manual jobs cannot connect to Unity Catalog
options.C: Job configurations are not versioned or tracked, leading to environment drift and no reproducibility
options.D: Manual jobs do not support Delta Live Tables

questions.explanation: The core problem with manual UI-based job creation is that configurations are not stored in version control. This leads to: configuration drift between environments (dev job looks different from prod), no audit trail, no rollback capability, and difficulty reproducing deployments. DAB stores all configurations in Git-versioned YAML. Job performance (A), Unity Catalog connectivity (B), and DLT support (D) are unrelated to the deployment method.

questions.text: Which of the following capabilities does Databricks Asset Bundles provide that Databricks Repos does NOT?

options.A: Viewing notebook change history
options.B: Cloning a GitHub repository into the workspace
options.C: Deploying job definitions, cluster configurations, and permissions across multiple environments using CLI commands
options.D: Connecting a workspace to a GitHub or GitLab repository

questions.explanation: Repos provides Git sync (A, B, D) — viewing history, cloning repos, and connecting to Git providers. What Repos cannot do is define and deploy jobs, pipelines, cluster configs, environment-specific settings, and permissions as code across multiple workspaces. That is the domain of Asset Bundles.

questions.text: A data engineering team wants to set up a GitHub Actions workflow that automatically validates and deploys a DAB bundle to staging when a pull request is opened against the `main` branch. What is the correct sequence of CLI commands in the workflow?

options.A: `bundle deploy → bundle validate → bundle run`
options.B: `bundle init → bundle deploy → bundle validate`
options.C: `bundle validate → bundle deploy --target staging`
options.D: `bundle plan → bundle init → bundle deploy`

questions.explanation: The standard CI/CD sequence is: first `bundle validate` (check config is correct), then `bundle deploy --target <env>` (deploy to the environment). `bundle run` (A) executes an already-deployed resource. `bundle init` (B, D) is a one-time scaffolding command not used in ongoing CI/CD pipelines. The validate-then-deploy sequence is the recommended pattern.

questions.text: In a CI/CD pipeline for Databricks Asset Bundles, what is the recommended authentication method for production deployments?

options.A: Hardcoded personal access tokens (PATs) in the workflow YAML
options.B: Username and password stored as GitHub secrets
options.C: Workload Identity Federation using a service principal (no secrets required)
options.D: OAuth U2M (User-to-Machine) authentication

questions.explanation: Databricks recommends Workload Identity Federation for CI/CD authentication. It uses OIDC tokens (no long-lived secrets needed), authenticates via `DATABRICKS_AUTH_TYPE: github-oidc` with a service principal's client ID. This is more secure than PATs (A) or passwords (B). OAuth U2M (D) is for interactive/human use, not automated pipelines.

questions.text: When `mode: development` is set on a deployment target in `databricks.yml`, which of the following behaviors is automatically applied?

options.A: All job schedules run immediately and continuously
options.B: All schedules and triggers are PAUSED, resources are prefixed with `[dev username]`, and concurrent runs are enabled
options.C: The bundle is deployed to all targets simultaneously
options.D: DLT pipelines are set to `continuous: true` automatically

questions.explanation: Development mode applies these automatic behaviors: (1) prepends `[dev <short_name>]` to resource names, (2) adds `dev` tag, (3) PAUSES all schedules and triggers, (4) enables concurrent job runs, and (5) sets DLT pipelines to `development: true`. Schedules are paused (not running continuously as in A). Bundles deploy to one target at a time (C). DLT `continuous` mode is not automatically enabled (D).

questions.text: A data engineer defines the following in `databricks.yml`: ```yaml variables: env_name: default: development targets: prod: mode: production variables: env_name: production ``` When running `databricks bundle deploy --target prod`, what value will `${var.env_name}` resolve to?

options.A: `development` (the default always takes precedence)
options.B: `production` (target-level variables override defaults)
options.C: An error is thrown because the variable is defined twice
options.D: `prod` (the target name is used automatically)

questions.explanation: Variable resolution follows a defined precedence order. Target-specific variables (defined within the `targets` section) override the top-level default values. So when deploying to `prod`, `${var.env_name}` resolves to `production`. The default `development` only applies when no higher-priority value exists. Defining the same variable in multiple places is normal and expected — not an error.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the `lookup` field in a DAB variable definition?

options.A: It looks up the variable value from a secrets vault
options.B: It allows the variable to reference another variable's value
options.C: It automatically retrieves the ID of an existing workspace resource by name
options.D: It looks up the current user's permissions for the resource

questions.explanation: The `lookup` field enables a variable to auto-resolve the ID of an existing workspace object by its name. For example, a variable with `lookup: { cluster: "my-cluster" }` automatically retrieves the cluster's ID. Supported types include clusters, jobs, pipelines, SQL warehouses, dashboards, and alerts. This is NOT a secrets lookup (A), cross-variable reference (B), or permissions check (D).

questions.text: A team wants a Databricks bundle job to run as a service principal in production, even though it was deployed by a human user. Which configuration achieves this?

options.A: Set the `--run-as` flag when running `databricks bundle deploy`
options.B: Define `run_as: service_principal_name: prod-sp` in the production target configuration
options.C: Grant the human user admin rights on the workspace
options.D: Create a separate bundle specifically for service principal deployments

questions.explanation: The `run_as` configuration (either at the top level or within a specific target) specifies the identity (user or service principal) under which deployed jobs/pipelines execute. This separates the deploy identity from the run identity. Setting it in the `prod` target block means production deployments run as the service principal. The `--run-as` CLI flag does not exist (A). Admin rights (C) don't set the run identity. Separate bundles (D) are unnecessary for this use case.

questions.text: A data engineer sets the following permissions in `databricks.yml`: ```yaml permissions: - level: CAN_MANAGE group_name: platform-team - level: CAN_VIEW group_name: data-consumers ``` What does this configuration achieve?

options.A: These permissions apply only to the `databricks.yml` file itself in Git
options.B: The `platform-team` group gets full control and `data-consumers` can view all resources deployed by this bundle
options.C: The permissions apply only to jobs, not pipelines
options.D: This configuration is invalid — permissions can only be set per individual resource

questions.explanation: Top-level `permissions` in `databricks.yml` apply to ALL resources deployed by the bundle. `CAN_MANAGE` grants full control (edit, delete, run), `CAN_VIEW` allows viewing resource details. Permissions apply to all resource types including jobs, pipelines, dashboards, etc. (not just jobs as in C). They control Databricks workspace resources — not the Git file (A). Top-level permissions ARE valid syntax (D is wrong).
